ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific term for garlic butter prawn as a prepared dish; closest parent 'shrimp food product' (FOODON_03302757) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns as a commodity group; no specific prepared garlic butter prawn entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 identifies prawn as a substance; no prepared garlic butter prawn concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of garlic butter prawn in Singapore is approximately 250 g, comprising whole or halved prawns cooked in garlic-infused butter sauce, served as a shared seafood dish.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked Prawns (Shrimp), stir-fried with butter and garlic — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Crustaceans, shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175180) with butter contribution modelled at 15 g per 100 g edible portion
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact garlic butter prawn entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) as the primary protein-mineral base, with butter (USDA FDC ID 173410) contribution modelled at approximately 15 g per 100 g edible portion to account for the garlic-butter sauce. Garlic contributes negligible macros at typical usage levels. Energy, fat, and saturated fat are adjusted upward accordingly. This is an estimated composite; values should be verified against laboratory analysis if used clinically.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for garlic butter prawn as a prepared dish. GI is estimated at 5 based on the negligible carbohydrate content of cooked prawns (approximately 0 g digestible starch per serving) and the established principle that high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate seafood dishes have a GI effectively near zero. Analogous reference: cooked shrimp and similar crustacean dishes are classified as low-GI foods in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 250 g serving calculated as (5 × 1.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 0.19, rounded to 0.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from butter and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of garlic butter prawn due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g, which can contribute significantly to daily sodium targets of 2000 mg; requesting reduced salt and sauce at restaurants is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content with negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Garlic butter prawn is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as its carbohydrate content is approximately 1.5 g per 100 g and its estimated glycemic index is very low (GI ≈ 5), resulting in minimal postprandial glucose excursion; however, the saturated fat from butter warrants attention in the context of insulin resistance management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from butter and moderate dietary cholesterol from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume garlic butter prawn in moderation given its estimated saturated fat content of approximately 5.9 g per 100 g from butter and dietary cholesterol of approximately 165 mg per 100 g from prawns; the omega-3 fatty acids naturally present in prawns provide a partial cardioprotective benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Butter Prawn (蒜蓉牛油虾) is a popular Chinese-style seafood dish widely served in Singapore's seafood restaurants and zi char stalls. The dish consists of whole or halved fresh prawns wok-fried at high heat in a generous amount of butter infused with finely minced or sliced garlic, often finished with white pepper, salt, and chopped spring onions. The cooking technique produces prawns with a lightly caramelised, aromatic garlic-butter coating while retaining a firm, juicy, and slightly springy texture. The flavour profile is rich, savoury, and intensely garlicky with a characteristic buttery sweetness. Nutritionally, garlic butter prawn is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with very low carbohydrate content, making it glycemically benign. The protein content of approximately 17.5 g per 100 g supports muscle maintenance, while the dish provides meaningful amounts of selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and iodine from the prawns. The butter component contributes saturated fat (approximately 5.9 g per 100 g) and elevates the sodium content, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the dish is well-tolerated due to its negligible glycemic load. It is commonly ordered as a shared dish in a multi-course Chinese seafood meal and is a signature offering at Singaporean seafood restaurants such as those along Boat Quay and East Coast Seafood Centre.

confidence_reason: No exact garlic butter prawn entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional nutrition database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) with butter contribution modelled at 15 g per 100 g edible portion. GI is estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database for low-carbohydrate crustacean dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base ingredient (cooked shrimp) is well-characterised in USDA SR Legacy and the butter modelling is methodologically defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites; no exact laboratory analysis for garlic butter prawn as a prepared dish was available in approved sources at time of processing. Values should be verified against HPB Singapore Foodo if a matching entry becomes available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp source entry and is not modelled from the butter component due to variability. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175180 with butter modelling; these are not -1 as defensible estimates exist. 4. The emoji in the original food_name ('Garlic Butter Prawn 👍') has been removed to produce a canonical English food name. 5. cuisine_tags in the input included 'Crab' and 'Lobster' which are not relevant to this dish and have been excluded. 6. GI is set to 5 as an analogous estimate for a very-low-carbohydrate crustacean dish; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 7. Halal status is marked true as prawns are generally considered halal in mainstream Islamic jurisprudence; however, some schools differ — consumers should verify with the serving restaurant. 8. Price data from field survey data (SGD 30.80) is preserved in source metadata but not included in the nutrient record as it is outside the scope of this workflow.
